title: Deuteronomy 2
subtitle: The Wilderness Years: Edom, Moab, Ammon, and the First Victories

sections.header: Verses 1–25 — Passing Through Edom, Moab, and Ammon

heb.gloss: your brothers the sons of Esau
heb.paragraph: The kinship language is theologically significant: Edom are Israel's brothers. Israel must not despise or provoke them. The covenant community's obligations of decency extend even to nations outside the covenant.

The repeated divine gift formula drives the chapter. God has allocated territories to Edom, Moab, and Ammon just as he has to Israel. The theology of territorial gift is universal: God is sovereign over all nations' inheritances.

refs.note: "'Did I not bring Israel up from Egypt, the Philistines from Caphtor and the Arameans from Kir?'" Amos expands Deuteronomy 2's theology: God orchestrates the migrations and inheritances of all nations, not just Israel.
"He marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands." Paul's Areopagus speech draws directly on Deuteronomy 2's theology of divinely allocated national boundaries.

notes.note: MacArthur: "Do not provoke them to war, for I will not give you any of their land." The restraint commanded toward Edom, Moab, and Ammon is a covenant lesson in proportionality — not every adversary is an enemy to be destroyed. Knowing who to fight requires knowing who not to fight.

MacArthur: The parenthetical about the Emites and Horites establishes that God had already been clearing the land of its former inhabitants through other nations. The conquest of Canaan is not an anomaly in God's governance but the continuation of a long providential pattern.

MacArthur: "Thirty-eight years passed until all that generation of fighting men had perished from the camp, as the LORD had sworn to them." The sentence is devastating in its brevity. Thirty-eight years: an entire generation buried in sand. The judgment at Kadesh was not reversed, not shortened, not negotiated. God's word stands.

MacArthur: "See, I have given into your hand Sihon the Amorite... Begin to take possession of it." God gives the victory before the battle — the gift precedes the effort. Faith acts on the promised outcome, not the present circumstances.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The command to pay Edom for food and water reflects the ancient Near Eastern custom governing the movement of armies through foreign territory. Israel must behave as a law-abiding traveller, not an invader — God had not commissioned them to take that territory.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: The parenthetical notes about pre-Israelite giant peoples (Emim, Horim, Zamzummin) suggest the Deuteronomic editor had access to traditions about Transjordan's pre-Israelite inhabitants. God had been doing this work of national displacement for generations before Israel arrived.

Tigay: The JPS commentary highlights that this section's legislation has parallels in other ANE legal collections but is distinctively reframed within the Deuteronomic theology of covenant love and centralised worship.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrewʿad-midrāk kap-rāgel("even the space of a foot") is an idiom for the smallest possible land portion. God allocated Edom to Esau's descendants — Israel has no claim despite ethnic kinship.
NET Note: The "thirty-eight years" is stated with chronological precision rare in the Pentateuch, anchoring the narrative in historical time.
Calvin: God's provision during forty years of wilderness wandering demonstrates that divine sustenance is sufficient. God allows want to teach dependence, then supplies to teach gratitude.
Calvin: The command not to harass Moab reveals that God's sovereignty extends to all nations. The land belongs to God, and he distributes it according to his purposes.

hist.context: Deuteronomy 2 recounts thirty-eight years of wilderness wandering compressed into a few verses, then traces Israel's path around the territories of Edom, Moab, and Ammon. God explicitly forbids Israel from taking any land belonging to these three nations — each has received its inheritance from God just as Israel has received Canaan. Israel is to pay for food and water — behaving as peaceful guests, not conquerors. Then the tone shifts: Sihon the Amorite is a different case. God hardens Sihon's heart (v.30) and Israel's victories begin.

sections.header: Verses 26–37 — Sihon Defeated: The Conquest Begins

heb.paragraph: Divine hardening language appears here as in the Exodus narrative. The theological tension is productive: Sihon refused from his own hardness of heart, yet God was sovereign over that refusal. Human agency and divine sovereignty are both affirmed.

One of Deuteronomy's key words. Israel's relationship to the land is not conquest in the ordinary sense but inheritance — receiving what God has given. The possession of Canaan is simultaneously military action and covenant fulfilment.

refs.note: "You gave them kingdoms and nations... They took over the country of Sihon king of Heshbon and the country of Og king of Bashan." Nehemiah's great prayer recites Israel's wilderness victories as acts of pure divine grace.

"He struck down many nations and killed mighty kings — Sihon king of the Amorites, Og king of Bashan." The Psalter's liturgical recitation of Sihon's defeat shows how this victory became a cornerstone of Israel's worship testimony.

notes.note: MacArthur: Israel's peaceful overture to Sihon is not weakness but diplomacy — it reflects genuine respect for the possibility of peaceful coexistence. God does not call Israel to make war where peace is possible. The refusal was Sihon's choice and God's sovereign appointment simultaneously.

MacArthur: "The LORD your God had made his spirit stubborn and his heart obstinate in order to give him into your hands." The hardening of Sihon parallels the hardening of Pharaoh. In both cases, the hardening does not create a new disposition but confirms an already existing rebellion. God does not make neutral people enemies — he firms up those who have already chosen enmity.

MacArthur: The ḥērem applied to Sihon's towns is best understood within the framework of divine judicial execution: God, whose patience is infinite, had waited 400 years (Gen 15:16) while the Amorites' wickedness ripened to judgment. Israel was not acting in self-interest but as the instrument of a patient judge whose time had finally come.

MacArthur: "There was not one town too strong for us." This is faith's summary statement — not of Israel's military capability, but of God's absolute reliability. Every city that appeared impregnable fell before the God who gave his word.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The hardening of Sihon's heart is structurally parallel to the Exodus hardening of Pharaoh and serves the same theological function: demonstrating that no human will can thwart God's redemptive purposes. The mighty are brought low to show God's greatness, not Israel's military skill.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: The JPS commentary notes thatḥēremis a technical term for irrevocable dedication of conquered populations to God — a sacral, not military, category.
Tigay: "From Aroer to Gilead" precisely defines the conquered territory with geographical specificity meaningful to an audience familiar with Transjordan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The phrasedibrê šālômshows Israel first offered peaceful passage — warfare was provoked by Sihon's refusal, not initiated by Israel.
NET Note: The dual expression uses two different Hebrew roots for hardening, emphasising the totality of God's sovereign act.
Calvin: God hardened Sihon's spirit — the same language used of Pharaoh. Sihon's refusal was genuine, but God used it to deliver Amorite territory to Israel.
Calvin: The total destruction of Sihon's cities is not arbitrary cruelty but covenantal judgment. The Amorites had been given 400 years of patience (Gen 15:16); their iniquity was now complete.

hist.context: Moses sends a message of peace to Sihon of Heshbon: allow Israel to pass through, purchasing food and water. Sihon refuses — God has hardened his spirit so that Israel might take his territory as the first of many inheritances. The ensuing battle results in complete victory: all towns taken, the population devoted to destruction. The territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok becomes Israel's first inheritance. Moses uses this victory as a theological marker: what happened to Sihon will happen to all the nations west of the Jordan.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Og↗ People
ppl.role: Giant king of Bashan; last of the Rephaim
ppl.text: Og king of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim — a pre-Israelite giant people. His iron bed, measuring nine cubits long (c.4.5m), was preserved in Rabbah of Ammon as a monument (Deut 3:11). Israel defeated him at Edrei after his defeat of Sihon, and Moses divided his territory among the Transjordan tribes. Deut 3 presents his defeat as proof that God can give Israel the whole land: if the giant Og fell, so will the Anakites in Canaan. He appears in numerous later biblical summaries of God’s power (Ps 135, 136; Neh 9).

ppl.name: Sihon↗ People
ppl.role: Amorite king of Heshbon; defeated by Israel
ppl.text: Sihon king of the Amorites ruled from Heshbon in Transjordan. When Israel requested peaceful passage through his territory, he refused and came out to fight (Num 21:21–25). Israel defeated him decisively, occupying his territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok. This victory is retold as a paradigm of God’s power in Deut 1–2, Ps 135:11, and Ps 136:19. His defeat, alongside Og’s, became a standard example of God’s faithfulness to Israel in later liturgical tradition.

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (14th–13th c. BC)
src.quote: Klaus Baltzer (1960) and Meredith Kline (1963) demonstrated that Deuteronomy follows the six-part structure of Hittite vassal treaties: preamble (1:1–5), historical prologue (1:6–3:29), stipulations (4–26), document clause (27:1–9), witnesses (31:19–22), and blessings/curses (28). This structure is attested only in 14th–13th century BC treaties, strongly supporting Mosaic authorship.
src.note: The treaty-form evidence is the most powerful external argument for Deuteronomy’s early date. Subsequent Assyrian vassal treaties (7th c. BC) have a different form, making a late (Josian) dating of the book structurally improbable.

src.title: Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC)
src.quote: The Neo-Assyrian VTE contains curse language strikingly similar to Deut 28, leading some scholars (Weinfeld, Steymans) to argue Deuteronomy was modelled on Assyrian treaties. However, the VTE represents only the curse section; Deuteronomy’s full six-part structure matches only Hittite treaties.
src.note: The parallel demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern covenant forms were widespread, but the direction of dependence is disputed. The Hittite parallel is structurally closer and temporally earlier, supporting the traditional dating.

src.title: Ugaritic and Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Terminology
src.quote: The vocabulary of Deuteronomy —berît(covenant),ahavah(love as covenant loyalty),hesed(loyal love), ‘adon’ (lord) — is attested in Ugaritic and other ANE diplomatic texts as standard covenant terminology.
src.note: This shows that Deuteronomy’s language of love, loyalty, and service was not merely personal piety but carried legal-diplomatic weight: Israel’s relationship with God is structured as a formal suzerainty covenant.

rec.who: Origen, Homilies on Deuteronomy (3rd c. AD)
rec.text: "Origen read Deuteronomy christologically throughout, interpreting Moses’ farewell discourses as prefiguring Christ’s teaching, the “prophet like Moses” (18:15) as a direct prophecy of Christ, and the land as a figure of the soul’s heavenly inheritance."

hebtext.note: The kinship language is theologically significant: Edom are Israel's brothers. Israel must not despise or provoke them. The covenant community's obligations of decency extend even to nations outside the covenant.

The repeated divine gift formula drives the chapter. God has allocated territories to Edom, Moab, and Ammon just as he has to Israel. The theology of territorial gift is universal: God is sovereign over all nations' inheritances.

Divine hardening language appears here as in the Exodus narrative. The theological tension is productive: Sihon refused from his own hardness of heart, yet God was sovereign over that refusal. Human agency and divine sovereignty are both affirmed.

tx.title: LXX Deuteronomy and the MT divergences
tx.content: MTandLXXDeuteronomy diverge more significantly than other Pentateuchal books. The LXX adds explanatory expansions, especially in the blessings and curses (ch.28) and the Song of Moses (ch.32).
tx.note: The LXX Deuteronomy was translated with greater theological freedom than other books. It often interprets rather than translates, and has shaped the NT’s use of Deuteronomy (Paul cites LXX Deut in Rom 10 and Gal 3).

tx.title: Qumran and the Samaritan Pentateuch
tx.content: Deuteronomy is the most-copied book at Qumran (29 manuscripts), showing its centrality to Second Temple Judaism. TheSPDeuteronomy adds a tenth commandment mandating an altar on Gerizim (Exod 20:17b insert), the central SP polemic.
tx.note: The Nash Papyrus (c.150–100 BC) preserves the Decalogue and Shema from Deuteronomy — the oldest surviving Hebrew manuscript of any biblical text. It shows a mixed text-type combining MT and LXX features.

positions.name: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.proponents: The dominant critical view since de Wette (1805) identifies the “book of the law” found in 2 Kgs 22 with Deuteronomy, placing its composition in Josiah’s reign (c.621 BC). Deuteronomy on this view was written to legitimise Josiah’s centralisation reforms.
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.name: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.proponents: The Hittite suzerainty treaty structure (attested only in 14th–13th c. BC) strongly supports early composition. The book’s internal claims (1:1, 31:9,22,24) and NT citations (Jesus cites Deuteronomy three times in the temptation: Matt 4:4,7,10) support Mosaic origin.
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

themes.note: The Wilderness Years: Edom, Moab, Ammon, and the First Victories

coaching.tip: Israel bypassed Edom, Moab, and Ammon—related peoples with their own God-given territories (vv. 4-19). Not every nation was Canaan; kinship limited conquest. Esau and Lot's descendants received protection. Election didn't mean everyone else was fair game.

Sihon's defeat (vv. 24-37) proved what Canaan's kings would face. God 'hardened his spirit' (v. 30)—language echoing Pharaoh. The pattern repeated: resistance invited ruin. Transjordan victories previewed Canaan's conquest.
